module.title: CLOSURES & HIGHER-ORDER
module.subtitle: functions as values · closures · varargs
module.theory:
## Functions are values
In Lua a function is just a value, like a number or a string. That means you can store it in a variable, put it in a table, pass it to another function, or hand it back as a return value.

~~~lua
local function double(n)
  return n * 2
end

local f = double      -- store the function in another variable
print(f(21))          -- 42  (calling f calls double)
~~~

Writing `local function double(n) ... end` is the same as `local double = function(n) ... end`. The second form makes it obvious that the function is a value being assigned.

> INTEL — `double` (no parentheses) is the function itself. `double(5)` (with parentheses) **calls** it and gives you the result. Mixing these up is the #1 beginner slip.

## Passing functions as arguments (higher-order)
A **higher-order function** is one that takes another function as an argument, or returns one. This lets you write a routine once and feed it different behaviour.

~~~lua
local function apply(fn, x)
  return fn(x)        -- call whatever function we were handed
end

local function shout(s) return s .. "!" end
print(apply(shout, "go"))   -- go!
~~~

Here `apply` does not know or care what `fn` does. It just calls it. You decide the behaviour by choosing which function to pass in.

## Returning functions + closures
A function defined **inside** another function can remember the local variables that surrounded it, even after the outer function has returned. That captured, remembered variable is what makes it a **closure**.

~~~lua
local function make_adder(n)
  return function(x)
    return x + n     -- n is remembered from make_adder
  end
end

local add10 = make_adder(10)
print(add10(5))   -- 15
print(add10(1))   -- 11
~~~

`make_adder(10)` builds and returns a brand-new function whose private `n` is locked to 10. Call `make_adder(3)` and you get a different function with its own `n` of 3 — they do not interfere.

## A closure that keeps state (a counter)
Because the captured local is shared between calls, a closure can hold state that survives across calls — like a counter that goes up every time you call it.

~~~lua
local function make_counter()
  local count = 0
  return function()
    count = count + 1   -- there is no ++ in Lua
    return count
  end
end

local next_id = make_counter()
print(next_id())  -- 1
print(next_id())  -- 2
print(next_id())  -- 3
~~~

`count` lives on between calls because the inner function still holds a reference to it. Each fresh call to `make_counter()` gets its own independent `count`.

> WARNING — Remember Lua has no `++` and no `+=`. To bump a value you must write `count = count + 1`. Also, two separate counters made by two `make_counter()` calls do NOT share their `count` — each closure has its own.

## Varargs: functions that take any number of arguments
The three dots `...` in a parameter list mean "capture all remaining arguments here." Inside the function, `...` stands for that list of extra values.

~~~lua
local function add_all(...)
  local total = 0
  for _, v in ipairs({...}) do   -- {...} packs the args into a table
    total = total + v
  end
  return total
end

print(add_all(1, 2, 3, 4))   -- 10
~~~

The expression `{...}` collects the varargs into a normal (1-indexed) array, so you can loop over them with `ipairs`.

## Counting varargs with select("#", ...)
To ask **how many** arguments were passed, use `select("#", ...)`. The literal string `"#"` is the special signal that means "give me the count."

~~~lua
local function how_many(...)
  return select("#", ...)
end

print(how_many("a", "b", "c"))   -- 3
print(how_many())                 -- 0
~~~

`select("#", ...)` counts even `nil` holes correctly, which `#{...}` cannot always do — so prefer it when you truly need the count.

> INTEL — Lua arrays are **1-indexed**: the first element is `t[1]`, not `t[0]`. Keep that in mind whenever you turn `{...}` into a table and read its slots.

exercises.title: STORE THE ROUTINE
exercises.brief: A function is just a value.
exercises.prompt:
Functions are values you can put in a variable.

Define **run_twice(fn, x)** that calls `fn` on `x`, then calls `fn` again on that result, and returns the final value.
~~~lua
local function inc(n) return n + 1 end
run_twice(inc, 5)   -- 7   (5 -> 6 -> 7)
~~~
exercises.starter:
function run_twice(fn, x)
  -- TODO: call fn on x, then on the result
  return x
end

tests.name: run_twice(inc, 5) -> 7
tests.code:
local function inc(n) return n + 1 end
assert(run_twice(inc, 5) == 7, "5 -> 6 -> 7 should be 7")

tests.name: works with doubling
tests.code:
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
assert(run_twice(dbl, 3) == 12, "3 -> 6 -> 12 should be 12")

tests.name: works with strings
tests.code:
local function bang(s) return s .. "!" end
assert(run_twice(bang, "go") == "go!!")

exercises.lore: The same routine, run again on its own output.

exercises.title: APPLY
exercises.brief: Pass a function as an argument.
exercises.prompt:
Define **apply(fn, x)** that simply calls the function `fn` with the argument `x` and returns the result.
~~~lua
local function neg(n) return -n end
apply(neg, 8)   -- -8
~~~
exercises.starter:
function apply(fn, x)
  -- TODO: call fn on x
  return nil
end

tests.name: apply(neg, 8) -> -8
tests.code:
local function neg(n) return -n end
assert(apply(neg, 8) == -8)

tests.name: apply with a string function
tests.code:
local function up(s) return s:upper() end
assert(apply(up, "trace") == "TRACE")

exercises.lore: Hand the engine a routine; it runs whatever you give it.

exercises.title: MAKE ADDER
exercises.brief: Return a function that remembers a number.
exercises.prompt:
Define **make_adder(n)** that returns a NEW function. That returned function takes one argument `x` and returns `x + n`. The value of `n` is captured (a closure).
~~~lua
local add10 = make_adder(10)
add10(5)   -- 15
add10(1)   -- 11
~~~
exercises.starter:
function make_adder(n)
  -- TODO: return a function that adds n to its argument
  return nil
end

tests.name: make_adder returns a function
tests.code: assert(type(make_adder(10)) == "function", "make_adder must return a function")

tests.name: add10 adds ten
tests.code:
local add10 = make_adder(10)
assert(add10(5) == 15 and add10(1) == 11)

tests.name: two adders are independent
tests.code:
local add3 = make_adder(3)
local add100 = make_adder(100)
assert(add3(1) == 4 and add100(1) == 101)

exercises.hint: Inside make_adder, return function(x) return x + n end.
exercises.lore: Forge a tool; bake in its calibration.

exercises.title: COUNTER CORE
exercises.prompt:
Define **make_counter()** that returns a function. Each time the returned function is called it increases an internal count by 1 and returns the new count, starting at 1.
~~~lua
local next_id = make_counter()
next_id()   -- 1
next_id()   -- 2
next_id()   -- 3
~~~
exercises.solution:
function make_counter()
  local count = 0
  return function()
    count = count + 1
    return count
  end
end

tests.name: counts 1, 2, 3
tests.code:
local c = make_counter()
assert(c() == 1 and c() == 2 and c() == 3)

tests.name: each counter is independent
tests.code:
local a = make_counter()
local b = make_counter()
assert(a() == 1 and a() == 2 and b() == 1, "counters must not share state")

exercises.hint: Declare local count = 0 OUTSIDE the returned function, then count = count + 1 inside.
exercises.lore: A ticking register, sealed inside the closure.

exercises.title: MAP
exercises.brief: Apply a function to every element.
exercises.prompt:
Define **map(fn, arr)** that returns a NEW array where each element is `fn` applied to the matching element of `arr`. Do not change the original array.
~~~lua
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
map(dbl, {1, 2, 3})   -- {2, 4, 6}
~~~
exercises.solution:
function map(fn, arr)
  local out = {}
  for i = 1, #arr do
    out[i] = fn(arr[i])
  end
  return out
end

tests.name: doubles each element
tests.code:
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
local r = map(dbl, {1, 2, 3})
assert(#r == 3 and r[1] == 2 and r[2] == 4 and r[3] == 6)

tests.name: works with a string function
tests.code:
local function bang(s) return s .. "!" end
local r = map(bang, {"a", "b"})
assert(#r == 2 and r[1] == "a!" and r[2] == "b!")

tests.name: empty array gives empty array
tests.code:
local function id(x) return x end
local r = map(id, {})
assert(#r == 0)

exercises.hint: Loop i = 1, #arr and set out[i] = fn(arr[i]). Arrays are 1-indexed.
exercises.lore: Run the filter over the whole datastream.

exercises.title: VARARG SUM
exercises.brief: Add up any number of arguments.
exercises.prompt:
Define **sum_all(...)** that accepts any number of numbers and returns their total. With no arguments it returns 0. Use `{...}` to gather the arguments.
~~~lua
sum_all(1, 2, 3, 4)   -- 10
sum_all()             -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function sum_all(...)
  -- TODO: add up all the arguments
  return 0
end
exercises.solution:
function sum_all(...)
  local total = 0
  for _, v in ipairs({...}) do
    total = total + v
  end
  return total
end

tests.name: sum_all(1,2,3,4) -> 10
tests.code: assert(sum_all(1, 2, 3, 4) == 10)

tests.name: no args -> 0
tests.code: assert(sum_all() == 0)

tests.name: handles a single arg and negatives
tests.code: assert(sum_all(7) == 7 and sum_all(5, -2, -3) == 0)

exercises.hint: for _, v in ipairs({...}) do total = total + v end.
exercises.lore: Aggregate every packet on the wire.

exercises.title: ARG COUNT
exercises.prompt:
Define **arg_count(...)** that returns how many arguments it was given, using **select("#", ...)**.
~~~lua
arg_count("a", "b", "c")   -- 3
arg_count()                 -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function arg_count(...)
  -- TODO: return the number of arguments
  return 0
end

tests.name: three args -> 3
tests.code: assert(arg_count("a", "b", "c") == 3)

tests.name: no args -> 0
tests.code: assert(arg_count() == 0)

tests.name: counts nil holes too
tests.code: assert(arg_count(1, nil, 3) == 3, "select(#) counts nils")

exercises.hint: return select("#", ...) — the "#" is a literal string.
exercises.lore: Count the incoming signals before you decode them.

exercises.title: COMPOSE
exercises.prompt:
Define **compose(f, g)** that returns a NEW function. Calling that function with `x` should first apply `g`, then apply `f` to that result — i.e. it returns `f(g(x))`.
~~~lua
local function inc(n) return n + 1 end
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
local h = compose(dbl, inc)
h(5)   -- 12   (inc(5)=6, then dbl(6)=12)
~~~

tests.name: compose returns a function
tests.code:
local function id(x) return x end
assert(type(compose(id, id)) == "function")

tests.name: applies g then f
tests.code:
local function inc(n) return n + 1 end
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
local h = compose(dbl, inc)
assert(h(5) == 12, "inc(5)=6 then dbl(6)=12")

tests.name: order matters
tests.code:
local function inc(n) return n + 1 end
local function dbl(n) return n * 2 end
local h = compose(inc, dbl)
assert(h(5) == 11, "dbl(5)=10 then inc(10)=11")

exercises.hint: Return function(x) return f(g(x)) end — inner g runs first.
exercises.lore: Two routines welded into a single pipeline.
